description: B's test utterances (Block B2 step 7). 15 utterances against fixtures/graph-tcp.json, covering: clean correct, vague hand-wave, confidently wrong, multi-concept ramble, off-topic derail, plus one dodged-with-context case. Also A's policy test inputs and the seeds for the Block B4 adversarial scripts (rambler / confident-wrong / derailer). Optional `transcript` gives prior turns for utterances that need context (e.g. dodging a direct question).

utterances.text: The congestion window is a sender-side limit on how much unacknowledged data can be in flight at once, and it gets adjusted up or down based on ACKs and loss.
If the sender gets three duplicate ACKs for the same segment, it retransmits that segment immediately instead of waiting for the retransmission timer to expire.
TCP increases its window additively each round trip when things are going fine, but cuts it multiplicatively, like halving it, the moment it sees a loss - that's AIMD.
it like... speeds up until it doesn't, you know?
there's a threshold thing that changes how it grows, somehow
after a loss it kind of just... recovers and keeps going I think
Slow start doesn't actually grow the window at all - it keeps the window flat at one segment the whole time, right up until ssthresh is hit, and only then does it start increasing.
Packet loss happens when the receiver's buffer is full, so it just refuses new packets - that's literally what tells the sender to slow down.
Fast recovery means going all the way back to slow start and rebuilding the window from scratch after any packet loss.

So it starts slow then speeds up and also sometimes packets get lost and it has to like, resend them, and there's a threshold thing too I think, and then it goes back to increasing but slower after that.
The window doubles each round trip, and if three duplicate acks show up it resends without waiting, but if a timeout happens instead the whole window just resets and it starts over from slow start.

The window is basically how much data you can have out there unacknowledged, and when you lose a packet that's the signal something's wrong, so you cut back hard but then creep back up slowly, additive-increase-multiplicative-decrease or whatever it's called.
Wait, is this related to how Wi-Fi works? My Wi-Fi has been so bad lately.
Can we talk about something else, this is kind of boring.

transcript.text: So after slow start, the growth changes to something else.
wait, changes how exactly? what makes it switch?

utterances.text: I mean, it just switches, that's kinda just how TCP works, anyway what else do you want to know?
